steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: Grasby concluded the complementary products' advantage 'may be due to an outlier in the complementary product data.' The evidence must show that one product pair is driving the whole category's higher rate — and that removing it erases the advantage.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — advantage disappears when tortilla chips and salsa are removed: Complementary extended brands had higher purchasing rates overall, but the gap vanished once that single pair was excluded, confirming it was the outlier. ✓
  • Choice B — outlier is in substitutable products: Points to the wrong category; Grasby's conclusion is about complementary products. ✗
  • Choice C — equal initial purchasing rates: Contradicts the observed advantage for complementary products that Grasby was explaining. ✗
  • Choice D — reverses the relationship: Has substitutable brands outperforming complementary ones, the opposite of what the study found. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
